source_text: I am learning to drive.

reference_translations.text: Sto imparando a guidare.
reference_translations.notes: Progressive; most natural for an in-progress skill.

reference_translations.text: Imparo a guidare.
reference_translations.notes: Simple present, also fine.

reference_translations.text: Sto imparando di guidare.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: imparare governs a, not di. Anti-anchor for the di-family reach.

explanation: Imparare fixes a before an infinitive: imparare a guidare. The progressive sto imparando fits the English -ing but is not required.

source_text: She stopped smoking last year.

reference_translations.text: Ha smesso di fumare l'anno scorso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: L'anno scorso ha smesso di fumare.
reference_translations.notes: Time phrase fronted, equally natural.

reference_translations.text: Ha smesso a fumare l'anno scorso.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: smettere governs di. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Smettere governs di: smettere di fumare. Note the opposite family from continuare a; the stop/continue pair straddles the two preposition families.

source_text: I need a new dictionary for the course.

reference_translations.text: Ho bisogno di un dizionario nuovo per il corso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Mi serve un dizionario nuovo per il corso.
reference_translations.notes: The servire alternative sidesteps bisogno; grammatical and common.

reference_translations.text: Ho bisogno un dizionario nuovo.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the di after bisogno is not optional. Anti-anchor for the dropped-di calque.

explanation: Avere bisogno di: the di is part of the construction and English 'I need X' gives no hint of it. The beneficiary per il corso uses per's base sense.

source_text: My parents live in Milan but work in Switzerland.

reference_translations.text: I miei genitori vivono a Milano ma lavorano in Svizzera.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; the two place prepositions contrast inside one sentence.

reference_translations.text: I miei genitori abitano a Milano ma lavorano in Svizzera.
reference_translations.notes: abitare variant, same structure.

reference_translations.text: I miei genitori vivono in Milano ma lavorano in Svizzera.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: city takes a. Anti-anchor for the English in-everywhere.

explanation: One sentence, both sides of the size-class rule: a Milano (city), in Svizzera (country). An attempt that gets one right and one wrong is the diagnostic this item is for.

source_text: Tonight we are eating at Giulia's.

reference_translations.text: Stasera mangiamo da Giulia.
reference_translations.notes: The compact personal-da idiom.

reference_translations.text: Stasera mangiamo a casa di Giulia.
reference_translations.notes: Spelt-out form, equally correct.

reference_translations.text: Stasera ceniamo da Giulia.
reference_translations.notes: cenare for the evening meal; natural.

explanation: At someone's = da + person: da Giulia. English's possessive ('at Giulia's') has no Italian mirror; the whole meaning sits in the da.

source_text: The film starts at half past eight.

reference_translations.text: Il film comincia alle otto e mezza.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Il film inizia alle otto e mezzo.
reference_translations.notes: iniziare variant; mezzo/mezza both current for the half hour.

reference_translations.text: Il film comincia a otto e mezza.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: clock times need the article inside the contraction (alle). Anti-anchor.

explanation: Clock time is a + article, fused: alle otto e mezza. The article is load-bearing; bare a otto is the standard miss.

source_text: I have been living in Rome for five years.

reference_translations.text: Vivo a Roma da cinque anni.
reference_translations.notes: Present tense + da: the ongoing-duration pattern.

reference_translations.text: Abito a Roma da cinque anni.
reference_translations.notes: abitare variant.

reference_translations.text: Ho vissuto a Roma per cinque anni.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG for this meaning: grammatical Italian, but it says the Roman years are over. The English perfect progressive means they are not. Anti-anchor for tense and preposition at once.

explanation: Still-running duration takes da with the PRESENT tense: vivo a Roma da cinque anni. English hides this in 'have been living'; the Italian present plus da does all of that work. Per five years + passato prossimo would close the period.

source_text: The lesson finishes in twenty minutes.

reference_translations.text: La lezione finisce fra venti minuti.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: La lezione finisce tra venti minuti.
reference_translations.notes: tra: identical.

reference_translations.text: La lezione finisce in venti minuti.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG for this meaning: in venti minuti says how long it takes, not when it will happen. Anti-anchor for the English in.

explanation: Time-until is fra/tra: fra venti minuti. The English 'in' is the trap; Italian's in + duration measures how long a task takes instead.

source_text: The train that comes from Naples arrives at ten.

reference_translations.text: Il treno che viene da Napoli arriva alle dieci.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Il treno proveniente da Napoli arriva alle dieci.
reference_translations.notes: Station-announcement register; the da is the same.

reference_translations.text: Il treno che viene di Napoli arriva alle dieci.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: venire takes da for origin; di marks identity/belonging, never the source of movement. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Origin with venire is da: viene da Napoli. And the clock time rides along as alle dieci; two preposition systems in one short sentence. (The bare noun-phrase 'il treno di Napoli' is attested colloquially, which is why the frame uses the verb: after venire the di form is unambiguously wrong.)

source_text: They are leaving for London tomorrow morning.

reference_translations.text: Partono per Londra domani mattina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Domani mattina partono per Londra.
reference_translations.notes: Fronted time, same content.

reference_translations.text: Partono a Londra domani mattina.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: partire fixes per for its destination. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Partire per: the departure verb fixes per for its destination, overriding the general a-for-towns instinct. Leave FOR maps directly, one of the few places English helps.

source_text: We go to school by bus or on foot.

reference_translations.text: Andiamo a scuola in autobus o a piedi.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; both means expressions in one breath.

reference_translations.text: A scuola andiamo in autobus o a piedi.
reference_translations.notes: Fronted; same prepositions.

reference_translations.text: Andiamo a scuola con l'autobus o a piedi.
reference_translations.notes: con l'autobus is heard colloquially; accept with a light note, but in autobus is the target pattern.

explanation: Vehicles take in (in autobus), feet take a (a piedi), and the destination keeps its own a scuola. Three a/in decisions in ten words; this is why means and motion drill together.

source_text: Anna's car is faster than Marco's.

reference_translations.text: La macchina di Anna è più veloce di quella di Marco.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; possession twice, comparison once, all on di.

reference_translations.text: L'auto di Anna è più veloce di quella di Marco.
reference_translations.notes: auto variant.

reference_translations.text: La macchina di Anna è più veloce che quella di Marco.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: comparison before a pronoun takes di, not che. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Di three times, doing two jobs: possession (di Anna, di Marco) and comparison (più veloce di). The quella stands in for the second macchina; che in its place is the recorded comparison miss.

source_text: I bought a wool jumper for my mother.

reference_translations.text: Ho comprato un maglione di lana per mia madre.
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Ho comprato per mia madre un maglione di lana.
reference_translations.notes: Reordered, same prepositions.

reference_translations.text: Ho comprato un maglione in lana per mia madre.
reference_translations.notes: in lana is catalogue register; acceptable, di lana is the neutral target.

explanation: Material is di: maglione di lana. The beneficiary is per's base sense: per mia madre. Two easy prepositions whose English counterparts (of? in? for? to?) refuse to map one-to-one.

source_text: Is there anything to drink?

reference_translations.text: C'è qualcosa da bere?
reference_translations.notes: Standard.

reference_translations.text: Avete qualcosa da bere?
reference_translations.notes: Asking people rather than the fridge; same da.

reference_translations.text: C'è qualcosa di bere?
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: purpose-infinitive is da. (Qualcosa di buono exists, but with an adjective, not an infinitive.) Anti-anchor.

explanation: Something-to-VERB is da: qualcosa da bere, niente da fare. The di+adjective pattern (qualcosa di buono) sits one door over and causes the crossover miss.

source_text: This book was written by a famous journalist.

reference_translations.text: Questo libro è stato scritto da un giornalista famoso.
reference_translations.notes: Standard passive.

reference_translations.text: Questo libro fu scritto da un giornalista famoso.
reference_translations.notes: Passato remoto passive; bookish but correct.

reference_translations.text: Questo libro è stato scritto per un giornalista famoso.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: per makes the journalist the audience, not the author. Anti-anchor.

explanation: The passive agent is da: scritto da un giornalista. English 'by' has no pull toward any Italian preposition, so this is pure learned mapping; per flips author into audience.

source_text: As a girl, she lived in the countryside.

reference_translations.text: Da bambina viveva in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; da + stage of life, imperfect for the ongoing past.

reference_translations.text: Da ragazza viveva in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: ragazza for the girlhood years; same construction.

reference_translations.text: Come bambina viveva in campagna.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: come compares; the stage-of-life da is the target. Anti-anchor for the as-calque.

explanation: Stage of life is da: da bambina. The feminine -a agrees with the subject. In campagna uses the familiar-space bare in, and the imperfect viveva carries the extended past.

source_text: Can you buy some milk and some apples?

reference_translations.text: Puoi comprare del latte e delle mele?
reference_translations.notes: Standard; the partitive agreeing twice.

reference_translations.text: Compri del latte e delle mele?
reference_translations.notes: Bare present as a request; same partitives.

reference_translations.text: Puoi comprare di latte e di mele?
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the partitive is di + ARTICLE; bare di is the dropped-article miss. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Some = articulated di, agreeing with each noun: del latte (m sg), delle mele (f pl). One English word, a whole agreement paradigm underneath.

source_text: The keys are on the kitchen table.

reference_translations.text: Le chiavi sono sul tavolo della cucina.
reference_translations.notes: Standard; two contractions.

reference_translations.text: Le chiavi sono sul tavolo da cucina.
reference_translations.notes: da cucina as the type-label (purpose da, like camera da letto); della cucina in ref 1 marks belonging. Both natural.

reference_translations.text: Le chiavi sono su il tavolo della cucina.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: su + il must fuse to sul. Anti-anchor for the uncontracted form.

explanation: Two contractions in sequence: su + il = sul tavolo, di + la = della cucina. Translation is where the formation drill has to fire un-cued.

source_text: Da studente, andavo all'università a piedi.

reference_translations.text: As a student, I walked to university.
reference_translations.notes: Natural English; the da-phrase becomes 'as a', the imperfect a habit.

reference_translations.text: As a student, I used to walk to university.
reference_translations.notes: used-to makes the habit explicit; equally right.

reference_translations.text: From a student, I walked to university.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: da here is stage-of-life, not from. Anti-anchor for the from-reflex.

explanation: Reading direction: da + person-noun at the head of a clause is the stage-of-life da, so 'as a student', never 'from'. A piedi renders as walked / on foot; folding it into the verb is the natural English move.

source_text: Il treno per Roma parte fra dieci minuti.

reference_translations.text: The train to Rome leaves in ten minutes.
reference_translations.notes: Natural English.

reference_translations.text: The train for Rome leaves in ten minutes.
reference_translations.notes: for-Rome is fine railway English too.

reference_translations.text: The train to Rome leaves for ten minutes.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: fra is time-until, which English gives as in. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Two prepositions that both look tricky and both land on plain English: per Roma is the destination of a departing service (to/for), fra dieci minuti is time-until (in). The English in that was a trap going one way is the answer coming back.

source_text: Ho comprato dei fiori e ho cominciato a preparare la cena.

reference_translations.text: I bought some flowers and started making dinner.
reference_translations.notes: Natural; the partitive surfaces as some, cominciare a folds into started + -ing.

reference_translations.text: I bought some flowers and began to prepare dinner.
reference_translations.notes: More literal; fine.

reference_translations.text: I bought of the flowers and started to prepare dinner.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: dei is the partitive some, not 'of the'. Anti-anchor for the literal read.

explanation: Dei fiori reads as some flowers; the of-the reading is the false friend. Cominciato a preparare loses its a in English, where start takes the -ing or the to-infinitive with no preposition.

source_text: Mi ha portato un bicchiere da vino, ma volevo un bicchiere di vino.

reference_translations.text: He brought me a wine glass, but I wanted a glass of wine.
reference_translations.notes: The whole point in one line: da = made-for, di = full-of.

reference_translations.text: She brought me a wine glass, but I wanted a glass of wine.
reference_translations.notes: Subject gender is unmarked in the Italian; either is right.

reference_translations.text: He brought me a glass of wine, but I wanted a wine glass.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: the two halves are swapped; da vino is the empty purpose-built glass. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Da vino / di vino is the minimal pair: purpose (a glass FOR wine, i.e. a wine glass) versus contents (a glass OF wine). The sentence stakes the whole meaning on reading the two prepositions correctly.

source_text: Aspettiamo l'autobus da mezz'ora.

reference_translations.text: We have been waiting for the bus for half an hour.
reference_translations.notes: The da-durative forces the English perfect progressive.

reference_translations.text: We've been waiting for the bus for half an hour.
reference_translations.notes: Contracted; same.

reference_translations.text: We wait for the bus since half an hour.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: calquing da as since and keeping the present produces non-English. Anti-anchor.

explanation: The mirror of the en→it trap: Italian present + da must become English HAVE BEEN -ing + for. Neither language's surface survives the crossing; the learner has to translate the time-structure, not the words.

source_text: Sono di Genova, ma stasera vengo da Milano.

reference_translations.text: I'm from Genoa, but tonight I'm coming from Milan.
reference_translations.notes: Both English 'from', two different Italian relationships.

reference_translations.text: I am from Genoa, but this evening I am coming from Milan.
reference_translations.notes: Fuller form, same reading.

reference_translations.text: I'm from Genoa, but tonight I'm coming to Milan.
reference_translations.notes: WRONG: da with venire is the origin, not the destination. Anti-anchor.

explanation: Di Genova is identity (hometown), da Milano is movement (where tonight's journey starts). English flattens both to 'from', so the it→en direction tests whether the reader kept the two relationships apart at all.
